The resurrection of Jesus Christ is not presented in Matthew as a spiritual idea or metaphorical renewal. It is an event that breaks into the physical world with violent force—an earthquake shakes the earth, an angel whose appearance blazes like lightning descends from heaven, and armed guards collapse in terror. The stone is rolled away not for Jesus to escape, but so that witnesses can see the empty tomb and know that He has truly risen from the dead, just as He said He would. This is the pivot point of all history, and Matthew makes clear that it happened in space and time, observable and undeniable. The women are commanded not to be afraid, yet they run from the tomb in fear and great joy—an honest portrayal of how overwhelming it is to encounter the risen Christ. When Jesus Himself appears to them moments later, their response is instinctive: they grasp His feet and worship Him. This is not metaphorical devotion but physical contact with the resurrected body of God the Son.

What strikes with particular force is how Matthew handles the opposition. The chief priests and elders do not deny that something extraordinary has happened at the tomb. The guards have witnessed it. Instead, they manufacture a lie—disciples stole the body while the guards slept—and pay money to suppress the truth. This is the world's response to resurrection: not reasoned argument, but corruption and cover-up. Matthew tells us this false account circulates among the Jews to his own day, a reminder that even the most undeniable work of God meets with stubborn human resistance. The resurrection is not something people stumble upon by accident and then gradually come to believe. It must be proclaimed, testified to, deliberately chosen to be believed or deliberately rejected and replaced with a lie.

The disciples' final encounter with the risen Jesus on the mountain in Galilee shows the pattern that will define the age to come. They worship Him, and yet some doubt—faith and uncertainty coexist even in the presence of the risen Lord. But Jesus does not delay or hesitate. He declares that all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to Him. This is the staggering claim that follows resurrection: the One who was crucified, dead, and buried now holds absolute authority over all things. From this position of complete power, He commissions His followers with a task that will extend to the end of the age—to go to all nations, making disciples, baptizing them in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, teaching them obedience to everything He commanded. This is not optional or temporary. It is the standing order of the risen Christ to His church. And He seals it with a promise: "I am with you always, even to the end of the age." The resurrection is not finished business that He leaves behind. It is the foundation from which He reigns and the guarantee that accompanies His people in their mission until He returns.
